headline: The Printing Presses Act Still Controls Malaysian Media
context: Home Ministry renewed 247 publication permits in January 2026 under the Printing Presses and Publications Act 1984, which requires annual government approval for all print media.

cards.big: Every newspaper needs annual government permission to exist.
cards.sub: The PPPA 1984: the Minister can revoke any permit, no judicial review required.

cards.big: Section 13A: Minister's decision is final and cannot be questioned in court.
cards.sub: An ouster clause that survives despite constitutional free speech guarantees under Article 10.

cards.big: Malaysia ranks 107th in RSF Press Freedom Index. Below Timor-Leste.
cards.sub: Online media grew around the Act. The government responded with the CMA.

cards.big: PH manifesto 2018: repeal PPPA. In office: 'review.' Out of office: silence.
cards.sub: Both coalitions benefit from the power once they hold it.

cards.big: A free press licensed annually by the government is not a free press.
cards.sub: The permit system makes every editor a supplicant, regardless of the current Minister.

cards.big: The law's survival across five governments tells you everything about its utility to power.
